Student Reality Lab

Can Young Adults Afford Rent in Major U.S. Cities?

This story shows where rent takes the biggest share of income. It also shows whether more cities are crossing the 30% affordability stress line over time.

Claim: Rent is taking a growing share of income in many U.S. cities, pushing more young adults above the 30% affordability stress threshold.

Why This Matters

For students and recent graduates, the question is simple. Can a first full-time paycheck cover rent without crowding out everything else?

Start Here: Where Rent Takes the Biggest Bite

Loading affordability data...

Change the year to see which cities move above the 30% benchmark and how the ranking changes.

Higher bars mean more income goes to rent. The red 30% marker is the common affordability stress benchmark.

Read This First

Start with the top bar. It shows the most strained city in the selected year. Cities beyond the red line are above the 30% stress benchmark. Use the year control to see whether that pressure is spreading or easing.

Horizontal bar chart with cities on the y-axis and rent burden percentage on the x-axis. A red vertical marker shows the 30% affordability benchmark.

Scale note: the x-axis adjusts to the selected year so bar comparisons stay readable for that year.

Sources: Zillow ZORI rent data and U.S. Census ACS income data, merged in datasets/processed.json.

Second View: Is Rent Stress Spreading?

This view shows the share of tracked cities above the 30% threshold in each year, so you can tell whether this is a one-year spike or a broader pattern.

Line chart with year on the x-axis and percentage of tracked cities above the 30% rent burden threshold on the y-axis.

More Detail

How this measure works

Rent burden compares annual rent to annual income. This project combines Zillow ZORI rent data with U.S. Census ACS income data to estimate how much income rent consumes in each city. The 30% line is a common affordability stress benchmark.

What the 30% line means

Rent burden is the share of income that would go to rent. When that share goes above 30%, housing is widely treated as financially stressful because less income is left for savings and other essentials.

Why changing the year matters

The year selector turns this from a snapshot into a test. If the same cities stay above 30% across years, or more cities cross that line over time, the affordability problem is broader than one bad year.

Data limitations to keep in mind

These are city-level averages, so they do not capture neighborhood variation or each person's exact situation. The estimates also do not account for roommates or shared housing choices. This measure is best used to spot broad affordability patterns, not to predict one person's exact budget.

Student Takeaway

Can You Afford to Move Out?

Ask in plain English. Type your income and a city or rent amount — the bot calculates your rent burden and tells you where you stand.
